content_preview: We have developed a new, first generation mathematical model for the resting membrane potential,  $ E_m $, of the chondrocyte, based partly on the experimental data obtained and described in section E
The equation governing the transmembrane potential, V, of the chondrocyte is
$$ \begin{align*}C_m\frac{dV}{dt}=&-(I_{K-DR}+I_{K-Ca}+I_{K-2p}+I_{K-ATP}+I_{Na,b}+I_{K,b}\\&+I_{Cl,b}+I_{NaK}+I_{NaCa}+I_{TRPV4})\tag{1}.\end{align*} $$

where  $ C_m $ is chondrocyte capacitance (8 pF). This equation includes all transport processes that are electrogenic i.e., generate net transmembrane current(s).

The intracellular concentrations of

The intracellular concentrations of Na+, [Na+]i and K+,
[K+]i, are governed by two transport equations, namely,
$$ \frac{d[Na^{+}]_{i}}{dt}=\frac{-(I_{Na,b}+3I_{NaK}+3I_{NaCa}-I_{NaH})}{\nu o l_{i}F} $$
and
$$ \frac{d[K^{+}]_{i}}{dt}=\frac{-(I_{K,b}-2I_{NaK}+I_{K-DR}+I_{K-2p}+I_{K-Ca}+I_{K-ATP})}{\nu o l_{i}F} $$

where  $ vol_i $ is the internal volume of the chondrocyte (calculated to be 0.005884 mL), and  $ F $ is the Faraday constant, 96,485 C/mol. Note that Equation (1) does not include  $ I_{NaH} $, since

Intracellular Ca²⁺ concentration, [Ca²⁺]ᵢ, also changes with time, due to transmembrane transport of Ca²⁺ by the Na⁺-Ca²⁺ exchanger and ATP-dependent Ca²⁺ pump, and intracellular calmodulin binding, a
$$ \frac{d\left[Ca^{2+}\right]_{i}}{dt}=\frac{\left(I_{NaCa}-I_{Ca,ATP}\right)}{\nu o l_{i}F}-0.045\frac{d\mathbf{O}_{c}}{dt} $$
where  $ O_c $ is the fraction of intracellular calmodulin bound to  $ Ca^{2+} $ (see Equation 39).
